---
title: "Chapter 39 — 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 38 · 🧭 Search & Problem Solving](ch38.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [40 · 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing →](ch40.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KRR) is the branch of AI concerned with encoding what a system *knows* in an explicit, symbolic form, and then mechanically deriving new facts from it. It is the engine room of classical "symbolic" or "good old-fashioned" AI: where machine learning extracts patterns from data, KRR encodes facts and rules and *reasons* over them with logical guarantees. It matters because so much human expertise is propositional ("if the patient has a fever and a rash, consider measles"), and because the resulting inferences are inspectable, verifiable, and explainable in a way that neural networks rarely are.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical & symbolic AI · used to encode facts/rules and derive guaranteed conclusions · the one key idea: separate *knowledge* (declarative facts) from *inference* (a general reasoning procedure).
:::

## 39.1 — Logic as a representation language

The deepest idea in symbolic AI is that you can write down knowledge as **logical sentences** and let a general-purpose procedure grind out their consequences. The knowledge is *declarative*: you state *what* is true, not *how* to compute anything. A separate **inference engine** decides what else must be true. This separation is powerful — change the facts and the same reasoner produces new conclusions, with no reprogramming.

The simplest such language is **propositional logic**. Atomic statements (called **propositions**) are either true or false — $\text{Raining}$, $\text{SprinklerOn}$ — and you combine them with connectives: $\neg$ (not), $\land$ (and), $\lor$ (or), $\Rightarrow$ (implies), $\Leftrightarrow$ (iff). A sentence like $\text{Raining} \Rightarrow \text{WetGrass}$ is a constraint on the world. Given a **knowledge base** (KB) — a set of such sentences — reasoning asks: does some query sentence $\alpha$ follow?

The precise notion is **entailment**, written $KB \models \alpha$: in *every* assignment of truth values that makes the KB true, $\alpha$ is also true. Think of each truth assignment as a possible world; entailment says $\alpha$ holds in *all* the worlds the KB allows. Entailment is about meaning (semantics). **Inference** is the syntactic procedure that tries to establish it — pushing symbols around according to rules. We want inference to be **sound** (only derives entailed sentences — never lies) and **complete** (derives every entailed sentence — never misses one). Soundness keeps you honest; completeness keeps you thorough.

The brute-force way to check entailment is **model checking**: enumerate every truth assignment and verify that wherever the KB is true, $\alpha$ is true too. With $n$ propositions there are $2^n$ rows, so this is exponential — a hint of why richer logics and smarter algorithms matter.

```python
# Entailment by truth-table enumeration (propositional).
from itertools import product

def entails(symbols, kb, query):
    # kb, query: functions mapping a {symbol: bool} model -> bool
    for vals in product([False, True], repeat=len(symbols)):
        model = dict(zip(symbols, vals))
        if kb(model) and not query(model):   # KB true but query false -> counterexample
            return False                      # one counterexample kills entailment
    return True                               # query held in every model of KB

syms = ["Rain", "Wet"]
kb    = lambda m: (not m["Rain"] or m["Wet"]) and m["Rain"]   # Rain=>Wet, and Rain
query = lambda m: m["Wet"]
assert entails(syms, kb, query)   # Wet is entailed
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Entailment is "truth in all models"; satisfiability is "truth in some model." They are duals: $KB \models \alpha$ exactly when $KB \land \neg\alpha$ is *unsatisfiable*. Most automated reasoners exploit this by trying to derive a contradiction rather than checking every world directly.
:::

## 39.2 — First-order logic: objects, relations, and quantifiers

Propositional logic is too coarse for real knowledge: to say "all humans are mortal" you would need a separate proposition for every human who ever lived. **First-order logic** (FOL, also called predicate logic) fixes this by talking about *objects*, *relations* among them, and *functions* — and crucially adding **quantifiers** that range over objects.

The vocabulary has three kinds of symbol. **Constants** name objects ($\text{Socrates}$); **predicates** name relations or properties that are true or false of objects ($\text{Human}(x)$, $\text{Loves}(x,y)$); and **functions** map objects to objects ($\text{MotherOf}(x)$ returns an object, not a truth value). The universal quantifier $\forall$ ("for all") and existential $\exists$ ("there exists") let one sentence range over an entire domain:

$$\forall x \; \big(\text{Human}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x)\big), \qquad \text{Human}(\text{Socrates})$$

From these two, FOL inference derives $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$ — the canonical syllogism, now mechanized. The expressive jump is enormous: one quantified rule replaces what would be infinitely many propositional ones, one per object in the domain.

That power has a price. FOL is **semidecidable**: if $\alpha$ is entailed, a complete procedure will eventually confirm it, but if it is *not* entailed the procedure may run forever, never able to say "no." There is no general algorithm that always halts with a yes/no answer. This is a fundamental wall (the Church–Turing undecidability result for first-order validity), and it shapes the whole field — practical systems deliberately trade expressiveness for decidability (see description logics in §39.6).

::: {.callout-warning}
A common confusion: $\forall x\,\exists y\,\text{Loves}(x,y)$ ("everyone loves someone, possibly a different someone each") is *not* the same as $\exists y\,\forall x\,\text{Loves}(x,y)$ ("there is one specific person whom everyone loves"). Quantifier order changes meaning entirely — swapping $\forall$ and $\exists$ is one of the most frequent modeling bugs.
:::

## 39.3 — Resolution: a single rule that proves everything

How do you mechanize FOL inference soundly *and* completely with just one inference rule? The answer is **resolution**, the algorithm behind most automated theorem provers and behind logic-programming languages like Prolog. The trick is proof by contradiction: to prove $KB \models \alpha$, you *assume the opposite* — add $\neg\alpha$ to the KB — and show the combined set is unsatisfiable by deriving the **empty clause**, a clause with no literals that signals a flat contradiction.

First, everything is converted to **conjunctive normal form** (CNF): an AND of **clauses**, where each clause is an OR of **literals** (a predicate or its negation). Any logical sentence can be mechanically rewritten into this shape, so nothing is lost. The **resolution rule** then says: if one clause contains $P$ and another contains $\neg P$, the two cannot both be fully satisfied by $P$'s value alone, so you may merge them, cancelling that complementary pair:

$$\frac{(A \lor P) \qquad (\neg P \lor B)}{(A \lor B)}$$

In FOL there is one more ingredient: **unification**. Before two literals can resolve, you find a substitution of variables that makes them syntactically identical — unifying $\text{Loves}(x, \text{Mary})$ with $\text{Loves}(\text{John}, y)$ via the substitution $\{x/\text{John},\, y/\text{Mary}\}$. Repeatedly resolving until you reach the empty clause is called a *refutation*, and resolution is **refutation-complete**: if a contradiction is logically present, resolution is guaranteed to find it eventually.

```python
# Propositional resolution refutation: derive empty clause from CNF.
def resolve(ci, cj):                       # clauses are frozensets of literals like "P","-P"
    out = []
    for lit in ci:
        comp = ("-" + lit) if not lit.startswith("-") else lit[1:]   # the complement of lit
        if comp in cj:
            out.append((ci | cj) - {lit, comp})   # merge both clauses, drop the cancelling pair
    return out

def refutes(clauses):                       # True if clause set is unsatisfiable
    clauses = set(map(frozenset, clauses))
    while True:
        new = set()
        cl = list(clauses)
        for i in range(len(cl)):
            for j in range(i + 1, len(cl)):
                for r in resolve(cl[i], cl[j]):
                    if not r:               # empty clause = contradiction found
                        return True
                    new.add(frozenset(r))
        if new <= clauses:                  # no new clauses -> stuck -> satisfiable
            return False
        clauses |= new

# KB: (Rain=>Wet)=(-Rain v Wet), and Rain ; prove Wet by adding its negation -Wet
assert refutes([{"-Rain", "Wet"}, {"Rain"}, {"-Wet"}])
```

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["FOL sentences<br/>KB and ¬α"] --> B["Convert to CNF<br/>(clauses)"]
    B --> C["Pick two clauses<br/>with complementary literals"]
    C --> D["Unify + resolve<br/>→ new clause"]
    D --> E{"Empty clause?"}
    E -- yes --> F["Contradiction:<br/>KB ⊨ α proven"]
    E -- no --> C
```

## 39.4 — Rule-based and expert systems: forward vs backward chaining

Full FOL resolution is expensive, so much practical symbolic AI restricts itself to **Horn clauses** — rules of the form $\text{IF } A_1 \land A_2 \land \dots \Rightarrow C$, with at most one positive conclusion. That "at most one conclusion" restriction is exactly what buys efficient, *polynomial-time* inference, and it is the basis of **rule-based systems** (also called **production systems**) and the classic **expert systems** of the 1970s–80s — MYCIN diagnosed bacterial infections, DENDRAL inferred molecular structures.

Such a system has three parts: a **knowledge base** of rules, a **working memory** of facts currently believed true, and an **inference engine** that decides which rules to apply. There are two directions to run the engine, and the choice is mostly about which end you start from.

**Forward chaining** is data-driven. You start from the known facts, fire every rule whose conditions are already met, add the conclusions to working memory, and repeat until nothing new appears (a state called a **fixpoint**). It is natural when you already have data and want to know everything it implies — monitoring, configuration, real-time alerting.

**Backward chaining** is goal-driven. You start from a query (the **goal**), find rules that could conclude it, and recursively try to prove each of their premises as sub-goals. This is how Prolog and most diagnostic systems work — you ask a question and the engine only ever explores what is relevant to answering it, ignoring the rest of the knowledge base.

| Aspect | Forward chaining | Backward chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Facts → conclusions | Goal → supporting facts |
| Driven by | Available data | The question asked |
| Best when | Many facts, open-ended consequences | Specific query, focused proof |
| Risk | Derives many irrelevant facts | Re-derives same sub-goals; can loop |
| Typical use | Real-time monitoring, alerting | Diagnosis, Prolog queries |

```python
# Forward chaining over Horn rules until no new fact appears (a fixpoint).
rules = [(["fever", "rash"], "measles"),
         (["measles"], "notify_clinic")]
facts = {"fever", "rash"}

changed = True
while changed:                       # keep sweeping the rules until a full pass adds nothing
    changed = False
    for premises, concl in rules:
        if concl not in facts and all(p in facts for p in premises):
            facts.add(concl); changed = True
assert "notify_clinic" in facts      # measles fired first, then cascaded to notify_clinic
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: few goals but many facts → **backward** chaining (don't compute what you weren't asked about). Few facts but many possible consequences → **forward** chaining. The classic **RETE** algorithm makes forward chaining scale by caching partial rule matches across cycles, so each fact added doesn't re-test every rule from scratch.
:::

## 39.5 — Structured knowledge: semantic networks, frames, ontologies, and knowledge graphs

Logic, as written so far, is flat — an unstructured pile of sentences. Humans, by contrast, organize knowledge into *structures*: categories, inheritance, typical properties with exceptions. A family of representations captures this structure directly, and they turn out to be notational sugar over logic plus some convenient default machinery.

A **semantic network** is a graph: nodes are concepts, edges are labelled relations. The two classic edges are $\text{is-a}$ (taxonomy, e.g. *Dog is-a Mammal*) and $\text{has-a}$ (composition / parts). **Inheritance** then falls out for free — if *Mammal has-property warm-blooded* and *Dog is-a Mammal*, then a *Dog* is warm-blooded without anyone stating it separately. Reasoning becomes graph traversal.

A **frame** is the object-oriented cousin of a network node: a named record with **slots** (attributes) and **fillers** (values), plus *defaults* that can be overridden lower down. A `Bird` frame might default `can-fly = true`, while a `Penguin` frame inherits from `Bird` but overrides that slot to `false` — exactly the **non-monotonic** "typically true, but with exceptions" reasoning that pure classical logic struggles to express (more in §39.7).

An **ontology** formalizes all this: a shared, explicit specification of the concepts in a domain and the relations among them, precise enough for a machine to reason over without ambiguity. On the web, ontologies are written in standardized languages. **RDF** (Resource Description Framework) represents every fact as a **triple** — $(\text{subject}, \text{predicate}, \text{object})$, e.g. $(\text{Socrates}, \text{isA}, \text{Human})$. Many triples sharing entities form a graph. **OWL** (Web Ontology Language) layers on top class hierarchies, property restrictions, and logical axioms (its formal underpinning is description logic, §39.6). Together they realize the **Semantic Web** vision: data published on the web, linked across sites, and machine-readable rather than just human-readable.

A modern **knowledge graph** is the industrial realization of this idea: a very large graph of entities and the relations between them (Google's Knowledge Graph, Wikidata, enterprise product/customer graphs) that powers search, question answering, recommendation, and — increasingly — *grounding* for LLMs, giving a model verified facts to lean on instead of hallucinating.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    Animal --> Mammal
    Animal --> Bird
    Mammal --> Dog
    Bird --> Penguin
    Mammal -. "warm_blooded=true" .-> Animal
    Bird -. "can_fly=true (default)" .-> Bird
    Penguin -. "can_fly=false (override)" .-> Penguin
```

The triple is the atom of a knowledge graph — a single labelled edge between two nodes. Strung together, triples that share entities form the graph a reasoner walks:

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 90" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <rect x="10" y="32" width="90" height="26" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="55" y="49" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">Socrates</text>
  <line x1="100" y1="45" x2="180" y2="45" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="140" y="38" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">isA</text>
  <rect x="180" y="32" width="80" height="26" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="220" y="49" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">Human</text>
  <line x1="260" y1="45" x2="330" y2="45" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="295" y="38" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">subClassOf</text>
  <rect x="330" y="32" width="80" height="26" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="370" y="49" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">Mortal</text>
</svg>

Reading the chain left to right: Socrates *is a* Human, Human *is a subclass of* Mortal — so a reasoner concludes Socrates is Mortal by following edges, the same syllogism from §39.2 now as a graph walk.

## 39.6 — Description logics: decidable reasoning for ontologies

There is a tension running through this whole chapter: propositional logic is decidable but weak, while full FOL is wonderfully expressive but only semidecidable. **Description logics** (DLs) carve out a deliberate sweet spot — a *decidable* fragment of FOL designed specifically for describing concepts and reasoning about how they relate. They are the formal heart of OWL, and the reason an OWL reasoner can promise to always terminate.

A DL knowledge base splits into two parts. The **TBox** (terminological box) holds general concept definitions — the schema or vocabulary, e.g. "a $\text{Parent}$ is a $\text{Person}$ who $\text{hasChild}$ some $\text{Person}$." The **ABox** (assertional box) holds facts about specific named individuals — "$\text{Anna}$ is a $\text{Parent}$." DLs build complex concepts from atomic ones using a small set of constructors: intersection ($\sqcap$, "and"), union ($\sqcup$, "or"), and restrictions over **roles** (the DL word for binary relations), such as $\exists \text{hasChild}.\text{Person}$ meaning "has at least one child who is a Person."

$$\text{Parent} \equiv \text{Person} \sqcap \exists \text{hasChild}.\text{Person}$$

The payoff is automated **classification** and **consistency checking**. A DL **reasoner** can compute the full subclass hierarchy *implied* by your definitions — discovering, say, that every `Parent` is necessarily a `Person` even if you never said so directly — and can flag definitions that contradict each other, all while *guaranteed to terminate*. That termination guarantee is precisely why OWL constrains its expressiveness so carefully: each OWL profile (EL, QL, RL) gives up a little expressive power in exchange for better worst-case reasoning complexity, letting engineers pick the decidable corner that matches their data scale.

## 39.7 — Reasoning under uncertainty, and the limits of pure logic

Classical logic is brittle in one specific way: it assumes facts are *certain* and rules have *no exceptions*. The real world offers neither — most knowledge is "usually true," and most facts come with some doubt. Two broad responses bridge the gap.

The first stays symbolic but relaxes a property called monotonicity. **Monotonic** reasoning means that adding new facts can only ever add conclusions, never retract them — but common sense violates this constantly: "birds fly" plus the new fact "Tweety is a penguin" should *retract* the earlier conclusion "Tweety flies." **Non-monotonic** logics (default logic, the closed-world assumption, circumscription) let conclusions be *defeated* by later information. This captures common-sense defaults and the frame-and-override pattern from §39.5, at the cost of giving up some of classical logic's clean guarantees.

The second response replaces crisp truth values with probabilities, connecting KRR to the probabilistic side of AI. Instead of asserting $\text{Disease} \Rightarrow \text{Symptom}$ as a certainty, you encode a conditional probability $P(\text{Symptom} \mid \text{Disease})$ and reason with **Bayesian networks** and other graphical models. Hybrid formalisms — **Markov logic networks**, **probabilistic soft logic** — attach a numeric weight to each first-order rule, so that violating a rule is merely *penalized* rather than strictly forbidden, blending logical structure with statistical inference.

Stepping back, the strengths and weaknesses of symbolic KRR come into focus. Its great virtues are **interpretability** (every conclusion carries a traceable proof you can audit), **guarantees** (soundness, plus explicit consistency checks), and **data efficiency** (a handful of rules can encode what a neural network would need millions of examples to learn). Its great weakness is the **knowledge-acquisition bottleneck**: a human must hand-author the rules and ontologies, which is slow, expensive, and error-prone — and the result is **brittle**, failing ungracefully the moment a situation falls just outside what was explicitly encoded.

This is precisely why the field is reviving in hybrid form. **Neurosymbolic AI** pairs neural perception and learning (which excel at messy, high-dimensional, ambiguous data) with symbolic reasoning (which excels at structure, composition, and hard guarantees) — for example, letting an LLM *propose* candidate symbolic facts and a logic engine *verify and combine* them. The aim is to keep the explainability and rigor of symbols while shedding the hand-coding burden, and it is one of the most active frontiers of AI today.

## 39.x — Key takeaways

- KRR separates **declarative knowledge** (facts/rules) from a general **inference engine** — change the knowledge and reuse the same reasoner.
- **Entailment** ($KB \models \alpha$) is truth in all models; good inference is **sound** (no false claims) and **complete** (misses nothing); usefully, $KB \models \alpha$ iff $KB \land \neg\alpha$ is unsatisfiable.
- **Propositional logic** is decidable but coarse; **first-order logic** adds objects, relations, functions, and quantifiers but is only **semidecidable**.
- **Resolution** plus **unification** gives a single refutation-complete proof rule; restricting to **Horn clauses** yields efficient polynomial-time rule-based systems.
- **Forward chaining** is data-driven (facts→conclusions); **backward chaining** is goal-driven (query→proof). Choose by which end is smaller.
- **Semantic nets, frames, ontologies, RDF/OWL, and knowledge graphs** add structure, inheritance, and defaults; **description logics** are the decidable fragment powering OWL (TBox schema + ABox facts).
- Pure logic is brittle and assumes certainty; **non-monotonic logics** handle exceptions and **probabilistic** formalisms handle uncertainty. The standing weakness is the **knowledge-acquisition bottleneck**, which **neurosymbolic AI** now attacks.

## 39.y — See also

- **Chapter 13 — Probabilistic Graphical Models**: Bayesian and Markov networks for reasoning under uncertainty, the probabilistic counterpart to logical KBs.
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning**: the formal machinery behind the probabilistic reasoning referenced in §39.7.
- **Chapter 22 — Graph Neural Networks**: learning over the same entity–relation graphs that knowledge graphs represent.
- **Chapter 38 — Search & Problem Solving**: the search procedures underlying inference engines and theorem provers.
- **Chapter 40 — Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing**: logical action models and constraint reasoning built on these representations.
- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models** and **Chapter 47 — Frontier & Emerging Directions**: the neurosymbolic revival that couples LLMs with symbolic reasoning.
- **Chapter 44 — Explainable AI & Interpretability**: contrasts symbolic transparency with the post-hoc explanation of neural models.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 38 · 🧭 Search & Problem Solving](ch38.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [40 · 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing →](ch40.html)
